perturbation.original_text: Company shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any such -1- <PAGE> employee. Company shall not disclose Highly Confidential Information to any of its consultants, or other vendors, or agents.
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall not disclose Highly Confidential Information to any of its consultants, or other vendors, or agents.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'Company shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any such employee' reduces the accountability of the company for its employees' actions concerning confidential information. This introduces a legal risk, as California law holds employers responsible for creating policies to protect confidential information and reasonably overseeing employee behavior related to trade secrets. Eliminating the liability clause weakens this protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law (CUTSA) implies a duty for companies to take reasonable steps to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets by its employees. Removing the direct responsibility for employee breaches weakens the agreement's enforceability under CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly held the company responsible for breaches of the agreement by its employees. The changed text omits this responsibility. The perturbation explanation states that this omission reduces accountability for employees' actions concerning confidential information and creates a legal risk. The law explanation says California law (CUTSA) implies a duty for companies to take reasonable steps to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets by its employees. The scraped law snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' and 'Improper means,' which includes breach of a duty to maintain secrecy. By removing the company's responsibility for employee breaches, the modified text weakens the company's obligation to prevent such breaches, thereby potentially contradicting the implied duty under CUTSA to take reasonable steps to prevent misappropriation by employees. Thus, the omission creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives are requested or required by applicable Federal or State law of the United States to disclose any of the Confidential Information, it is agreed that the Receiving Party or its Representative, as the case may be, will provide the Furnishing Party with prompt notice of such request(s) so that it may seek an appropriate protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance by the Receiving Party or its Representative with the provisions of the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives are requested or required by applicable Federal or State law of the United States to disclose any of the Confidential Information, the Receiving Party or its Representative may furnish that portion (and only that portion) of the Confidential Information which it is legally compelled to disclose.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for 'prompt notice' eliminates the opportunity for the Furnishing Party to seek a protective order. This affects their ability to protect confidential information, potentially violating California trade secret laws, which require reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law (CUTSA) requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. Eliminating the 'prompt notice' provision removes a significant protection, potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable measures' standard.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the 'prompt notice' requirement, which the law explanation argues is a 'reasonable measure' to protect trade secrets, as required by California law. The original text ensured the Furnishing Party could seek a protective order. The changed text allows the Receiving Party to disclose confidential information without notice, potentially compromising trade secrets and failing to meet the 'reasonable measures' standard. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' which could occur if the Receiving Party discloses the trade secret without the opportunity for the Furnishing Party to protect it. This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction definition because it omits a legally important step for protecting confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties hereby represent and warrant that the undersigned officer is authorized to execute this Agreement on its behalf.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the representation and warranty regarding the officer's authorization to execute the agreement introduces legal uncertainty. This is a problem, as California law requires that all parties entering a contract are authorised to do so and able to represent their party.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1556 states that "All persons are capable of contracting, except minors, persons of unsound mind, and persons deprived of civil rights.". By removing the parties representations, it makes the contract less enforceable.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1. NATURE OF A CONTRACT [1549 - 1615] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Parties [1556 - 1559] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) 1556. All persons are capable of contracting, except minors, persons of unsound mind, and persons deprived of civil rights. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original text contains a representation and warranty that the officer signing the agreement is authorized to do so. The changed text omits this representation entirely. The Perturbation Explanation states that California law requires all parties entering a contract to be authorized to represent their party. The Law Explanation references California Civil Code Section 1556, which states that all persons are capable of contracting. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms this code. Removing the warranty makes the contract less enforceable by removing a check that the person entering the contract is authorized to do so, creating a legal loophole. This aligns with the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction.
